text: The surgeon begins a minimally invasive Ivor Lewis esophagectomy laparoscopically. A liver retractor is placed, and the gastropathic ligament is incised. Dissection proceeds toward the esophageal hiatus, identifying the right crus. The frontal esophageal ligament is incised, and dissection continues through the esophageal hiatus into the mediastinum. The esophagus is retracted anteriorly, revealing the left crus.

The surgeon focuses on the greater curve of the stomach, incising the gastrocolic ligament to enter the lesser sac. Careful dissection preserves the right gastroepiploic artery and vein. The surgeon uses a bipolar device to divide the short gastric arteries and veins near the stomach fundus. Loose attachments between the stomach and pancreas are severed, allowing complete mobilization of the stomach from the retroperitoneum. The stomach is reflected anteriorly, revealing the left gastric pedicle, where lymph nodes are swept up toward the specimen. The left gastric artery and vein are divided using a linear stapler.

The surgeon performs a standard pyloroplasty incision, dividing the pyloric ring. The incision is then closed transversely with four to five sutures, ensuring the mucosa is incorporated into each stitch.

The surgeon creates the gastric tube by dividing vessels along the lesser curve of the stomach using a vascular stapler. Sequential fires of a linear stapler are used to fashion the gastric tube, which is approximately five centimeters wide. Traction is applied to the fundus of the stomach toward the left hemidiaphragm to ensure proper alignment and prevent spiraling during stapler application.

After creating the gastric tube, the surgeon places a single stitch between the specimen and the gastric conduit. Both are then pulled into the chest following esophageal mobilization during thoracoscopy. The esophageal hiatus is examined, and a single stitch is placed to partially close it, reducing the risk of delayed herniation of abdominal contents into the chest.

During thoracoscopy, a suture is placed in the central tendon of the diaphragm and brought out through a separate incision in the chest wall. Downward traction on this suture provides clear visualization of the distal thoracic esophagus within the inferior pulmonary ligament.

The surgeon uses a fan retractor to reflect the lung anteriorly. The inferior pulmonary ligament is divided with an ultrasonic device. The mediastinal pleura is divided superiorly toward the azagus vein, which is then identified, circumferentially mobilized, and divided using a vascular stapler.

The surgeon identifies and divides the right vagus nerve. Peri-esophageal lymph nodes are swept off the pericardium, with the dissection plane extending laterally toward the border of the right main stem bronchus.

The surgeon skeletonizes the right main stem bronchus and carries the subcarinal lymph node dissection superiorly toward the carina, ensuring identification to prevent injury to the posterior membranous airway. The left main stem bronchus is also skeletonized, completing the nodal dissection. The mediastinal pleura posterior to the esophagus is incised, with careful identification and preservation of the thoracic duct and overlying pleura. Small branches of the thoracic duct and aortoesophageal branches are identified and divided between clips.

The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus toward the thoracic inlet, maintaining a dissection plane close to the esophageal wall above the azygos vein to minimize injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve. Subsequently, the gastric specimen and conduit are pulled into the mediastinum.

The surgeon divides the esophagus just above the level of the azygos vein. A posterior port site is enlarged and protected with a laparoscopic wound protector for specimen extraction. The anvil of a 28mm stapler is placed into the chest and inserted into the lumen of the thoracic esophagus, secured with two concentric purse string sutures.

A gastronomy is created in the gastric conduit near the lesser curve staple line. The stapler is inserted into the gastric conduit, and excess conduit is pulled into the mediastinum. The stapler penetrates the greater curve of the stomach and docks to the anvil, firing to create a circular esophagogastric anastomosis. The gastronomy is closed with sequential firings of a linear stapler. The chest is irrigated, and a Jackson-Pratt drain and chest tube are placed adjacent to the anastomosis.
The lung is re-expanded, marking the completion of the esophagectomy procedure.
